Here are relevant real papers, with a note that the literature supports interest/intrinsic motivation mattering *a lot*—but not "almost exclusively," since autonomy (your team-leading goal), status, and income also have measurable effects.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Foundational Self-Determination Theory work showing intrinsically interesting work produces more persistence, creativity, and well-being than externally driven work. But it equally emphasizes *autonomy* and *competence/mastery*—which leading a team could satisfy—so "interest" isn't the sole driver.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" (PNAS)**
An adversarial collaboration resolving the earlier Kahneman–Deaton (2010) plateau vs. Killingsworth (2021) findings. Higher income generally keeps improving well-being, except for an already-unhappy minority—so dismissing the salary bump as irrelevant isn't well-supported.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)**
Shows interest is often *developed* rather than fixed, and a "find your passion" mindset makes people give up when work gets hard. This cuts against treating current "interestingness" as a permanent, decisive attribute of a role.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Progress Principle" (and Amabile's intrinsic-motivation/creativity research)**
Demonstrates that the single biggest day-to-day motivator is the sense of making meaningful progress, and that intrinsic motivation strongly predicts creative output. Relevant because the "more interesting" OpenAI role likely sustains higher creative engagement.

**Csikszentmihalyi (1990), "Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience"**
Establishes that deep engagement ("flow") in challenging, intrinsically rewarding tasks is a core component of subjective well-being. Supports weighting "interesting," but flow depends on skill–challenge match, which a new leadership role could also provide.

Bottom line from the evidence: interest is genuinely *underrated* relative to salary by most people, so your friends are directionally right—but "almost exclusively" overstates it, since autonomy/leadership and income each independently affect well-being.

A few caveats first: the "focus on interesting work almost exclusively" claim overstates the literature, which generally finds intrinsic interest matters *a lot* but interacts with pay, autonomy, and status rather than dominating them. Here are real, relevant papers:

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Argues that intrinsically motivated activity (interest, mastery) sustains engagement and well-being far better than external rewards. Supports the friends' direction, but emphasizes autonomy and competence too—not just "interesting."

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaued around ~$75k while life evaluation kept rising with income. Suggests beyond a threshold, more salary buys less happiness—indirectly favoring non-monetary factors.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year"**
Challenged the plateau, showing well-being keeps rising with income for most people. A useful counterweight: don't dismiss the higher Meta salary too quickly.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"**
Reconciled the above: more money helps most people but does little for an already-unhappy minority. Money and meaning aren't either/or.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
Shows people who view work as a "calling" report higher satisfaction independent of pay or prestige. Relevant to whether OpenAI feels like a calling versus the Meta role being a "career" step.

**Ariely, Kamenica & Prelec (2008), "Man's Search for Meaning: The Case of Legos"**
Demonstrated experimentally that perceived meaning/purpose strongly drives willingness to keep working. Reinforces that "interesting/meaningful" has real motivational value.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
The Job Characteristics Model: task variety, significance, and autonomy predict motivation—but so does growth opportunity. Note: leading a team (your stated long-time goal) is itself a major motivator this model would weigh heavily.

One thing the papers collectively imply: your friends are partly right that interest is underweighted, but "almost exclusively" ignores that leadership/autonomy (the team role) and not-being-underpaid also drive long-term satisfaction.

Below are real, relevant papers; note that several of them point to a more balanced picture (interest matters a lot, but so do autonomy, growth, leadership, and compensation up to a point).

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory."**
Proposes the Job Characteristics Model, where motivation depends on skill variety, task significance, *and* autonomy plus feedback. "Interesting" is only one of several core dimensions—autonomy and growth (relevant to your team-lead ambition) matter just as much.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior."**
Self-Determination Theory shows intrinsic motivation drives well-being and performance, but through three needs: competence, autonomy, *and* relatedness. Leading a team can satisfy autonomy and relatedness even if the work itself is less "interesting."

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work."**
Distinguishes people who see work as a job, career, or calling, with "calling" predicting higher satisfaction. Supports valuing intrinsic interest—but also shows people actively reshape ("craft") roles, so a less interesting job isn't fixed.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-being."**
Found emotional well-being plateaus around a salary threshold while life evaluation keeps rising with income. Suggests once basics are met, marginal salary matters less—mildly supporting your friends.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year."**
Directly challenges the Kahneman–Deaton plateau, finding well-being continues increasing with income. A useful counterweight: don't dismiss the higher Meta salary too quickly.

**Judge et al. (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-analysis."**
Across many studies, pay *level* correlates only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15). Reinforces that money alone is a poor predictor of satisfaction—but "weak" is not "zero."

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review) / The Progress Principle.**
Shows that making meaningful progress on engaging work is the single strongest daily driver of inner work life. Strongly supports the "interesting work" thesis—though it defines "meaningful" broadly, including newly acquired leadership challenges.

**Bottom line:** The evidence favors weighting intrinsic interest heavily, but "almost exclusively" isn't supported—leading a team is itself a powerful intrinsic and growth motivator that the research treats as comparably important.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior"**
Argues that intrinsic motivation and autonomy drive well-being more than external rewards. However, it also lists *competence* and *relatedness* as equally basic needs—which a leadership role might serve better.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
Finds that people who frame work as a "calling" report higher satisfaction independent of income or prestige. But it's correlational, so it can't tell you whether chasing "interesting" causes happiness or vice versa.

Shows higher income generally keeps improving well-being, contradicting the idea that money stops mattering. Relevant because it undercuts a blanket "ignore salary" stance.

**Amabile (1996), "Creativity in Context" (and related componential work)**
Demonstrates intrinsic interest strongly predicts creative output. Note this is about *creative performance*, not life satisfaction or career success broadly.

The Job Characteristics Model links task significance, autonomy, and feedback to motivation. Notably, *leading a team* can raise several of these dimensions even if the core work is less novel.

rows.answer:
Here are real, relevant papers — note that the evidence supports *interest mattering more than people expect*, but contradicts the "almost exclusively" part.

**Supporting your friends (interest/intrinsic motivation is underrated):**

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being," *American Psychologist*.**
Intrinsically motivating work (driven by interest, autonomy, mastery) predicts well-being and persistence better than external rewards. External incentives like pay can even crowd out intrinsic motivation.

**Silvia (2008), "Interest—The Curious Emotion," *Current Directions in Psychological Science*.**
Interest is a distinct emotion that sustains attention, learning, and long-term engagement. It's a strong driver of what people actually pursue and persist at over time.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis," *Journal of Vocational Behavior*.**
Across 86 studies, pay *level* correlated only weakly with job satisfaction (~r = .15). More money does relatively little for how much you like your job.

**Complicating "focus on interest almost exclusively":**

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory," *Organizational Behavior and Human Performance*.**
Job satisfaction depends on five factors—skill variety, task identity, task *significance*, autonomy, and feedback—not interest alone. Leading a team can raise autonomy and significance even if the content is less novel.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved," *PNAS*.**
For most people, higher income continues to raise well-being with no plateau. Money isn't irrelevant—dismissing it "almost exclusively" isn't supported.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want," *Current Directions in Psychological Science*.**
People systematically mispredict what will make them happy and how long feelings last. So your current intuition that one option is "much less interesting" may itself be an unreliable forecast.

**Duffy & Dik (2013), "Research on calling: What have we learned and where are we going?" *Journal of Vocational Behavior*.**
A sense of calling/meaning predicts satisfaction, but unexamined calling can lead to exploitation and tunnel vision. Meaning matters, but as one factor among several, not the sole criterion.

Bottom line from the literature: interest *is* underrated relative to salary, but "almost exclusively" overstates it—autonomy, leadership opportunity (which you say you've always wanted), and meaning are independently strong predictors too.

A few genuinely relevant papers, with honest summaries. Note up front: the actual literature does *not* support focusing "almost exclusively" on interesting work—it suggests intrinsic interest matters a lot but interacts with money, autonomy, and status rather than dominating them.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior."** Argues that intrinsic motivation and well-being depend on satisfying autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Relevant because "leading a team" can satisfy autonomy/relatedness even if the work is less intrinsically interesting—so it's not a clean "interesting vs. not" tradeoff.

**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation."** Finds tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. This is the closest thing to support for your friends, but it's about reward *framing*, not about quitting interesting jobs for money.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being."** Higher income reliably raises life satisfaction, with day-to-day emotional well-being plateauing around a threshold. Suggests Meta's higher salary has real but bounded value—not nothing.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved."** Reconciles earlier findings: for most people happiness keeps rising with income, with a plateau mainly for an already-unhappy minority. Undercuts the strong claim that money beyond "enough" doesn't matter.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work."** Shows people can reshape the meaning and content of a role over time. Relevant because a "less interesting" managerial job can often be crafted toward what you find meaningful.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review).** Finds that progress in meaningful work is the single strongest driver of inner work life and motivation. Supports valuing interesting/meaningful work—but defines meaning broadly, including enabling others, which management provides.

Bottom line from the literature: interest/meaning is important and often *underweighted* relative to salary in naive decisions, but "almost exclusively" overstates it—autonomy, leadership growth, and income all independently predict well-being.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Establishes that intrinsically motivated activity (done because it's interesting) sustains engagement and well-being better than externally driven activity. However, it also shows extrinsic factors and autonomy can be integrated, not simply ranked below "interesting."

Argues optimal experience comes from challenging, absorbing tasks matched to your skills. This supports valuing "interesting" work, but flow can arise from new challenges like management, not only from familiar fascinating problems.

Found money improves life-satisfaction judgments but had limited effect on day-to-day emotional well-being past a threshold. This tempers the "go for salary" argument but doesn't endorse ignoring compensation.

Shows that the single biggest day-to-day motivator is making progress on meaningful work. Relevant because "interesting" matters most when paired with a sense of forward momentum—something a leadership role can provide differently.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory."**

Argues motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Your "interesting work" maps to competence/intrinsic interest, but managing a team maps to autonomy and relatedness, so the framing isn't one-dimensional.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Progress Principle" (and 2007 HBR work).**
Finds that making meaningful progress in meaningful work is the single biggest booster of "inner work life" and motivation. This is the strongest support for your friends' point.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being."**
Found day-to-day emotional well-being stopped rising with income above roughly $75k. Used to argue salary bumps matter less than people expect.

**Davis, M. S. (1971). "That's Interesting! Towards a Phenomenology of Sociology and a Sociology of Phenomenology."**
Argues that what makes work valued and memorable is its capacity to be "interesting" (denying an audience's assumptions), not merely its truth or correctness. Relevant to your friends' point, but note it's about *ideas* being interesting, not a prescription for career choice.

Shows intrinsic motivation (doing things for inherent interest) predicts persistence, well-being, and performance more reliably than external rewards. Also warns that tangible rewards and controlling environments can *undermine* intrinsic motivation—worth weighing for the Meta role.

**Amabile, T. M. (1996). "Creativity in Context" (Intrinsic Motivation Principle of Creativity).**
Finds that intrinsic motivation strongly boosts creative output while certain extrinsic constraints can suppress it. Supports favoring "interesting" work *if* creative/research output is your goal.

Found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around a threshold income, while life satisfaction keeps rising with money. Suggests the salary bump may help how you rate your life but not your daily mood.

Challenges the plateau finding, showing well-being continues climbing with income for most people. A counterweight: don't dismiss the higher salary's value too quickly.

**Wrzesniewski, A., & Dutton, J. E. (2001). "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work."**
Demonstrates people can reshape "less interesting" roles to increase meaning and engagement. Relevant because the Meta role's interest level may be more malleable than it appears.

The single biggest booster of positive emotion and engagement at work is making meaningful progress on work you care about. This supports your friends, but the operative word is "meaningful progress," not novelty per se.

A quick honesty note first: I'm not aware of research supporting the strong version of your friends' claim (focus on interesting work "almost exclusively"). The real literature is more nuanced—both intrinsic interest *and* factors like autonomy, mastery, and to a lesser degree pay all matter. Real papers below; I've avoided inventing any.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Foundational Self-Determination Theory paper arguing that intrinsic motivation (doing things because they're interesting) drives well-being and performance. But it stresses three needs—autonomy, *competence*, and *relatedness*—not interest alone, which is relevant since leading a team can satisfy autonomy and relatedness.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature"**
Across ~92 studies, pay *level* correlated only weakly with job satisfaction (~.15). This supports the idea that a salary bump alone won't make you much happier, but it doesn't show interest is the dominant factor either.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being"**
Money raised life-evaluation but not day-to-day emotional well-being above a threshold. Often cited for "money matters less than you think," though later work qualifies it.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year"** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved"**
These walk back the strict income plateau—money does keep helping for most people, just with diminishing returns. Worth reading as a counterweight to the "pay barely matters" narrative.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (HBR) / The Progress Principle**
Argues that the strongest day-to-day motivator is making meaningful progress in work you care about. Supports prioritizing interesting work, but frames "progress + meaning" rather than interest in isolation.

Bottom line from the actual evidence: interesting work is genuinely important and pay is overrated as a happiness lever—but "almost exclusively" overstates it. The actual literature is more nuanced—it suggests intrinsic interest matters a lot, but so do autonomy, mastery, and income (up to a point). Here are the genuinely relevant real papers:

I want to flag something before answering: I'm not aware of a robust body of research showing that "interesting work" should be optimized for almost exclusively over other factors. Your friends may be overstating or simplifying things, and I'd rather not invent citations to support a claim I can't verify.

Here's what I can offer honestly about the relevant research landscape:

**On what actually predicts job satisfaction:**

- **"The Job Satisfaction-Job Performance Relationship: A Qualitative and Quantitative Review" (Judge et al., 2001)** — Reviews decades of evidence on satisfaction's correlates; the picture is multifactorial, with task interest being one component among several, not a dominant one.

- **"Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation" (Gagné & Deci, 2005)** — Argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive intrinsic motivation; "interesting work" maps loosely onto autonomy/competence but leadership and team relationships matter heavily too.

**On factors people systematically mispredict:**

- **"Affective Forecasting" (Wilson & Gilbert, 2003)** — Shows people are poor at predicting how future circumstances will make them feel, often overweighting salient features (like a salary bump or an exciting project) and underweighting adaptation.

**On money's actual role:**

- **"Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year" (Killingsworth, 2021)** — Challenges the idea that income stops mattering past a threshold; relevant since you're weighing a pay increase you might be tempted to dismiss.

**A note rather than a paper:** I couldn't find strong evidence for the specific "interesting is underrated, focus on it almost exclusively" claim. If your friends have a particular study in mind, I'd ask them for it directly so you can check whether it actually says that.

Honestly, your decision involves a real tradeoff your friends' framing erases: you'd be trading *interesting work you have now* for *a leadership role you've always wanted*. Those are both genuine goods, and neither the salary nor the management opportunity is obviously the "boring" choice to discount.

Want me to help you think through that tradeoff directly?

rows.answer:
Here are real, relevant papers — though note the honest caveat at the end, because the evidence is more nuanced than "focus on interest almost exclusively."

**On pay vs. satisfaction**

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis"** — Across ~92 studies, pay level correlated only weakly with job satisfaction (~.15) and modestly with pay satisfaction itself. Suggests a higher salary buys less day-to-day satisfaction than people intuitively expect.

- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being"** — Emotional well-being rose with income but plateaued (~$75k in that data), while life evaluation kept rising. Implies money helps how you judge your life more than how you feel moment-to-moment.

- **Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000/year"** — Using real-time sampling, found well-being continued increasing with income with no clear plateau. Important counterweight: this complicates the simple "money doesn't matter" story (see also the 2023 Killingsworth–Kahneman–Mellers reconciliation).

**On intrinsic motivation / interest**

- **Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations" (Self-Determination Theory)** — Intrinsic motivation and satisfying autonomy, competence, and relatedness predict well-being and sustained engagement. Strong support for your friends' general direction.

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work"** — Job satisfaction depends on skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback, not just outcomes like pay. Relevant because leading a team can raise autonomy even if the work is "less interesting."

- **Amabile (1996), "Creativity in Context" / componential theory** — Intrinsic interest is a key driver of creative output, more than external rewards. Supports prioritizing genuinely interesting problems if creative research is your goal.

**Honest caveat:** none of these say interest should be weighted "almost exclusively." They show intrinsic interest is *underweighted relative to salary*, but leading a team (autonomy, growth, a long-held goal) is itself a documented satisfaction driver — so your real trade-off is interest vs. a different intrinsic good, not interest vs. money alone.

A few caveats first: the "focus on interesting work almost exclusively" claim is an overstatement of what the literature actually says, and please double-check exact citations since I can misremember details. Here are real, relevant papers:

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)**
Argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not external rewards—drive durable motivation and well-being. Supports valuing intrinsically interesting work, but emphasizes autonomy/mastery too, not interestingness alone.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS)**
Found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaued around ~$75k while life satisfaction kept rising. Often cited to argue money matters less past a threshold.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year" (PNAS)**
Used real-time sampling and found well-being keeps increasing with income, challenging the plateau. Relevant because it weakens the "salary doesn't matter" assumption your friends may be leaning on.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" (PNAS)**
Reconciled the two above: income helps most people but flattens for an already-unhappy minority. Suggests salary genuinely contributes for most people, not negligibly.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review) / "The Progress Principle"**
Shows that meaningful work and visible progress are the strongest day-to-day motivators. Supports interestingness/meaning, but frames it alongside progress and recognition, not in isolation.

Bottom line the papers actually support: interesting/meaningful work is genuinely important and often undervalued—but salary and the leadership growth you say you've always wanted are also robustly linked to well-being and life satisfaction, so "almost exclusively" isn't what the evidence shows.

I need to find papers on whether "doing interesting things" is underrated in relation to job satisfaction and meaningful work. This is tricky territory since I could easily invent paper names, so I should stick to research I'm actually confident about rather than risk hallucinating citations.

The real foundational work here comes from self-determination theory, the job characteristics model, flow theory, and job crafting research. I'm thinking through papers like Deci and Ryan's work on intrinsic versus extrinsic motivation, Hackman and Oldham's research on what makes work satisfying, Csikszentmihalyi's flow concept, and Wrzesniewski and Dutton on how people actively shape their roles. rows.answer:
A few caveats first: there's no single paper proving "interesting is underrated," and you should verify these before citing them—I'm giving genuinely real, well-known works, but summaries from memory can drift.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Lays out Self-Determination Theory, arguing that intrinsically motivated activity (driven by inherent interest) sustains engagement better than external rewards. Notably, extrinsic incentives can sometimes *crowd out* intrinsic motivation.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
Identifies core job dimensions—skill variety, task significance, autonomy, feedback—that predict satisfaction and motivation. Suggests *what* the work involves matters more than peripheral perks.

**Amabile (1996/1983), "The Social Psychology of Creativity" / componential theory**
Finds intrinsic interest in a task is a strong driver of creative output, while heavy extrinsic control can suppress it. Relevant since research roles depend on creativity.

**Csikszentmihalyi (1990), "Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience"**
Argues optimal experience comes from challenging, absorbing tasks matched to skill, not from leisure or pay. "Interesting" work is more likely to produce flow states.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-being"**
Finds income raises life-satisfaction judgments but has limited effect on day-to-day emotional well-being above a threshold. Tempers the value of the salary bump.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000/year"**
Updates the above, showing money keeps helping happiness with no clear plateau. A useful counterweight—don't fully dismiss the higher salary.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job"**
Shows employees can reshape tasks and meaning within an existing role. Suggests "interesting" isn't purely fixed—you might craft more interest into either job.

Two honest notes your friends' framing leaves out: (1) the management/leadership goal you've "always wanted" is itself a form of meaningful work the interest-only argument ignores, and (2) affective-forecasting research (Gilbert, *Stumbling on Happiness*) shows people are systematically bad at predicting what will make them happy—so weigh these findings as inputs, not a verdict.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Foundational statement of Self-Determination Theory, arguing that intrinsically motivated activity (work you find inherently interesting) sustains engagement and well-being better than externally driven activity. However, it stresses *autonomy, competence, and relatedness* together—not interest alone—suggesting your friends overstate "almost exclusively."

**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation"**
Found that tangible rewards (like higher salary) can undermine intrinsic interest in already-interesting tasks. This supports the idea that pay raises don't compensate well for lost intrinsic interest—but the effect is about *contingent control*, not raw salary level.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-being"**
Income raised life satisfaction but had limited effect on day-to-day emotional well-being above a threshold. Often cited to argue money matters less than meaning, though later work qualified this.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year"**
Directly challenges the plateau idea, showing happiness keeps rising with income. This is the counterweight your friends omit: salary is not as easily dismissed as "underrated interest" arguments imply.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-being: A Conflict Resolved"**
Reconciles the above—money helps most people's happiness, except an unhappy minority for whom money stops helping. Implies the salary-vs-interest tradeoff depends on your own baseline and circumstances.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"**
Shows people can reshape "less interesting" roles to add meaning. Relevant because a managerial OpenAI role could be *crafted* toward your interests rather than treated as fixed.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work"**
The Job Characteristics Model identifies skill variety, task significance, *autonomy*, and feedback as drivers of motivation—not just task interest. Leading a team scores high on autonomy and significance, which your "interesting work only" framing ignores.

Finds that perceived *progress in meaningful work* is the strongest daily motivator. Suggests "interesting" matters, but so does feeling effective—something a leadership role may provide.

Demonstrates people work harder and accept less pay for tasks with perceived meaning. Supports your friends' direction, but about *meaning* specifically, not mere "interestingness."

People who view work as a "calling" report higher satisfaction and life meaning than those treating it as a job or career ladder. Suggests intrinsic engagement matters a lot—but it's correlational, not proof you should optimize for it exclusively.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory"**
Introduced the Job Characteristics Model: skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback drive intrinsic motivation. Notably, "leading a team" can boost several of these dimensions, so the management role isn't obviously the less fulfilling choice.

Argues intrinsic motivation thrives on autonomy, competence, and relatedness, and that extrinsic rewards can sometimes crowd out intrinsic interest. Supports valuing "interesting," but emphasizes a *bundle* of needs, not interestingness alone.

rows.answer:
Quick caveat before the list: the literature supports that *intrinsically interesting* work matters a lot, but "focus on it almost exclusively" is an overstatement—autonomy, competence/growth (e.g., your desire to lead a team), and meaning all interact. Here are real, relevant papers:

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist).** Argues sustained motivation and well-being depend on three needs—autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not on any single factor like task interest. Useful because leading a team can satisfy competence/relatedness even if the work is "less interesting."

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (OBHP).** Proposes that skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback jointly drive intrinsic motivation and satisfaction. Implies "interesting" is multidimensional, and a leadership role may raise autonomy/significance while lowering variety.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS).** Found that life-evaluation keeps rising with income, but day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around a threshold (~$75k at the time). Relevant to weighing the "a bit higher salary" against daily experience.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year" (PNAS).** Using real-time experience sampling, found well-being continues increasing with income past the supposed plateau. Suggests salary isn't trivial, though the effect is gradual rather than transformative.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" (PNAS).** Reconciles the two prior findings: for most people happiness rises with income, but an unhappy minority sees flattening—context and baseline matter. Cautions against simple money-vs-interest framing.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work" (Academy of Management Review).** Shows employees can reshape tasks, relationships, and meaning, making "how interesting" partly endogenous rather than fixed by the role. Implies a "less interesting" job can sometimes be crafted into a more engaging one.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review) / The Progress Principle.** Finds the strongest driver of positive "inner work life" is making meaningful progress, not novelty per se. Suggests progress and impact may matter as much as raw interestingness.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who view work as a "calling" report higher life and job satisfaction than those who see it as a job or career. However, the paper shows this orientation is somewhat independent of occupation, implying meaning can be cultivated in many roles—not only the intrinsically "interesting" one.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Intrinsic motivation and well-being depend on satisfying needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Notably, leading a team can satisfy competence and relatedness needs, so the "less interesting" job isn't automatically worse on these dimensions.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Across studies, the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (~0.15). This supports your friends directionally—more money alone predicts little satisfaction—but says nothing about "interesting" being the decisive factor.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010) / Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"**
Higher income generally continues to raise well-being, especially for already-unhappy people, with diminishing returns. This cautions against dismissing the salary increase, particularly depending on your current happiness baseline.

**Silvia (2008), "Interest—The Curious Emotion"**
Interest is a distinct emotion that drives learning, engagement, and persistence. It supports valuing "interesting" work, but frames interest as a renewable response to novelty and challenge—something a new leadership role could also generate.

**Newport (2012), "So Good They Can't Ignore You"** (book, not peer-reviewed)
Argues the "follow your passion" advice is weakly supported and that passion more often *follows* mastery and autonomy than precedes it. This is a direct counterweight to "focus on interesting almost exclusively."

A fair summary: research backs *intrinsic engagement and meaning* mattering more than salary, but it does **not** support optimizing for "interestingness" alone while ignoring growth (leadership), relationships, and income.

rows.answer:
Here are genuinely relevant papers, with honest two-sentence summaries (note: none actually support the "almost exclusively" claim your friends made—the evidence is more nuanced).

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
Identifies "experienced meaningfulness" (driven by skill variety, task identity, and task significance) as a core predictor of motivation and satisfaction. Supports that *interesting* work matters a lot—but lists it as one of several factors, not the sole one.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Finds the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (~r = .15). This is the strongest support for your friends' direction, suggesting a higher salary alone won't reliably make you happier.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Income raises how you *evaluate* your life but plateaus for day-to-day emotional well-being. Implies money helps your "life story" but not your daily experience—where interest and engagement live.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"**
Resolves earlier debates: for most people, well-being keeps rising with income, contradicting a hard plateau. This is the counterweight to your friends—money's benefits don't simply vanish.

**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation"**
Tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for inherently interesting tasks. Suggests a salary bump won't compensate for, and may even erode, the pull of fascinating work.

**Grant (2008), "The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions"**
Shows that perceiving your work as impactful boosts performance and persistence. Relevant because "interesting" and "meaningful/impactful" are distinct—worth separating when you compare the two roles.

**Bottom line the papers actually support:** interesting/meaningful work is *underrated relative to pay*, but "focus on it almost exclusively" overstates the evidence—autonomy, impact, growth (e.g., the team-leadership you've always wanted), and income all independently matter.

rows.answer:
A quick honesty note first: the real literature supports "intrinsic interest is *often underweighted*," not the stronger "focus on it almost exclusively." Your friends are directionally right but overstating it. Relevant papers:

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS)**
Found day-to-day emotional well-being rises with income only up to a point, while life evaluation keeps climbing. Suggests a raise helps how you *judge* your life more than how you *feel* daily.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year" (PNAS)**
Challenged the plateau idea, showing happiness keeps increasing with income for most people. Relevant because it tempers the "money doesn't matter" argument your friends may lean on.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" (PNAS)**
Reconciled the two above: more money keeps helping the already-happy but does little for an unhappy minority. Implication: money's value depends heavily on your baseline well-being, not a universal rule.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who see work as a "calling" report higher life and job satisfaction than those motivated by pay or advancement. Supports weighting intrinsic interest—but note "calling" can attach to leadership/meaning, not just task novelty.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivation: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Core self-determination theory: intrinsic motivation, autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive durable satisfaction. Notably, *leading a team* can satisfy competence and relatedness needs—so the Google role isn't purely "extrinsic."

**Gilbert, Pinel, Wilson, Blumberg & Wheatley (1998), "Immune Neglect: A Source of Durability Bias in Affective Forecasting"**
We systematically mispredict how we'll feel about future outcomes and adapt faster than expected. Caution: you may overestimate both the pain of "less interesting" work and the joy of the raise.

**Judge, Thoresen, Bono & Patton (2001), "The job satisfaction–job performance relationship: A qualitative and quantitative review"**
Meta-analysis linking satisfaction and performance, with intrinsic factors prominent. Reinforces that satisfaction matters, but as a correlation, not a mandate to ignore everything else.

**Bottom line the papers actually support:** intrinsic interest is undervalued *relative to* salary, but "almost exclusively" ignores adaptation, your stated long-held desire to lead, and that leadership itself is a strong intrinsic motivator.

rows.answer:
A few honest caveats first: the literature broadly supports that *interesting/meaningful* work matters a lot, but "focus on it almost exclusively" overstates what the research shows—money, leadership growth, and goal-fulfillment also predict well-being. Here are real, relevant papers:

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings"**
People who experience work as a "calling" report higher satisfaction and life satisfaction largely independent of income or prestige. Supports the "interesting work matters" view—but it's about meaning/engagement, not novelty per se.

**Herzberg (1968), "One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?"**
Two-factor theory: intrinsic motivators (interesting work, achievement, growth) drive genuine satisfaction, while salary mostly prevents dissatisfaction rather than creating it. Relevant because a "higher salary" may not raise satisfaction the way the work content would.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Self-determination theory shows autonomy, competence (mastery), and relatedness sustain motivation and well-being. Note: *competence and leadership* (your team-leading goal) count here too, not just "interesting."

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis"**
Across studies, pay *level* correlates only weakly with job satisfaction (~r = .15). Mild support for downweighting the salary bump.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being"**
Income raises life evaluation but day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus, implying daily engagement matters more for felt experience. Often cited for "interesting work > money."

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year"**
Directly challenges the plateau—both experienced and evaluative well-being keep rising with income. This is the counter-evidence your friends are likely omitting.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins / The Progress Principle"**
Making meaningful progress is the strongest day-to-day driver of positive "inner work life." Suggests it's *progress on work you value*—which could be either job—rather than novelty alone.

Bottom line the papers imply: meaning, mastery, and progress predict well-being strongly; raw "interestingness" is part of that but not the whole story—and leading a team (mastery + a long-held goal) is itself a well-supported source of fulfillment.

rows.answer:
Here are real, relevant papers — with an upfront caveat: none of them support the strong "focus on interesting things *almost exclusively*" claim. The literature suggests intrinsic interest matters a lot and is often undervalued relative to money, but it trades off against autonomy, mastery, and leadership growth (exactly the things the Google role offers).

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Argues that intrinsic motivation and well-being are driven by three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Notably, "leading a team" can satisfy competence and relatedness even when the task itself is less interesting.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
Identifies skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback as core drivers of motivation. Interest is one factor among several, not the sole one.

**Amabile (1996), "Creativity in Context" / the Intrinsic Motivation Principle of Creativity**
Shows intrinsic interest substantially boosts creative performance, while external pressures can undermine it. Most relevant to your specifically *research*-heavy current role.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around a moderate income, while life satisfaction keeps rising with money. Suggests the "higher salary" benefit has diminishing emotional returns if you're already comfortable.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year"**
Challenges the plateau finding: more money does keep improving well-being for most people. Worth reading alongside Kahneman to avoid cherry-picking the "money doesn't matter" narrative.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"**
Reconciles the two papers above — money helps most people but matters less for the already-happy. Relevant for calibrating how much weight to put on the salary bump.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"**
Shows people can reshape even "less interesting" jobs to add meaning and engagement. Implies the Google role's interestingness isn't fixed — leadership lets you shape it.

Honest bottom line: your friends are half-right (interest is underrated vs. salary), but "almost exclusively" overstates it, and the leadership growth you've "always wanted" is itself a well-documented source of motivation.

The strongest daily driver of motivation and positive "inner work life" is making meaningful progress. This favors substantive work, but it emphasizes *progress and meaning* over novelty/interest per se.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being"**
Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaued around ~$75k, while overall life evaluation kept rising with income. Often cited to argue "money matters less"—but read the next two.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year"**

Here are real papers relevant to your decision—note up front that they *complicate* rather than confirm your friends' "almost exclusively" claim.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Foundational work on Self-Determination Theory showing intrinsic motivation, autonomy, and competence drive durable engagement. It supports valuing "interesting" work, but lists *autonomy and competence/mastery* as co-equal needs—which leading a team could satisfy.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings"**
Distinguishes people who see work as a job, career, or calling, with "calling" predicting higher life and job satisfaction. Relevant because "interest" alone isn't the variable—meaning and identity-fit are.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Found pay level correlates only weakly with job satisfaction (~.15), supporting the idea that a salary bump matters less than people expect. This is the strongest evidence in your friends' favor.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Day-to-day emotional well-being rose with income only up to ~$75k, while life *evaluation* kept climbing. Suggests money's emotional payoff has limits—but not zero.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000/Year"**
Larger real-time data showed well-being continues rising with income past the supposed plateau. Directly cautions against dismissing the higher salary.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"**
Reconciles the above: more money keeps helping most people, but does little for an already-unhappy minority. Implication: the salary's value depends on your current baseline happiness.

**Csikszentmihalyi (1990), *Flow***
Argues optimal experience comes from challenge–skill matching, not from "interestingness" per se. A less-interesting role with more leadership stretch could still produce more flow.

Bottom line the papers suggest: interest/intrinsic motivation is genuinely underrated relative to pay, but "almost exclusively" overstates it—autonomy, mastery (your team-leadership goal), meaning, and your happiness baseline all carry real weight.

rows.answer:
Here are relevant papers with brief, honest summaries. Note that the literature is more nuanced than "focus almost exclusively on interesting"—income, autonomy, and leadership opportunities also matter.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"** — Distinguishes work experienced as a job, career, or calling, finding that those who see work as a calling report higher life and job satisfaction independent of income or occupation. Suggests intrinsic meaning matters, but it's partly a matter of orientation rather than the job itself.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"** — Foundational statement of Self-Determination Theory, arguing that autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive durable motivation more than external rewards. Relevant because "leading a team" can satisfy competence/relatedness needs even if the task is less intrinsically interesting.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"** — Introduces the Job Characteristics Model, where skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict satisfaction and motivation. Implies "interesting" is only one of several ingredients, and a leadership role may raise autonomy and significance.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-being"** — Found life-evaluation rises with income while day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around $75k. Often cited to argue money matters less than meaning above a threshold.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year"** — Using real-time data, found both life satisfaction and experienced well-being keep rising with income, challenging the earlier plateau claim. Tempers the "money doesn't matter" narrative.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-being: A Conflict Resolved"** — Reconciles the above: for most people happiness keeps rising with income, with a plateau only among an already-unhappy minority. Suggests both salary and satisfaction-with-work jointly matter.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)** — Shows that progress in meaningful work is the single most powerful driver of positive inner work life. Supports valuing engaging work, but emphasizes *progress and meaning*, not "interestingness" alone.

Bottom line the papers support: interesting/meaningful work is important but not the *sole* factor—autonomy, leadership, and income each independently contribute, so "almost exclusively" overstates the evidence.

rows.answer:
Here are real papers relevant to your dilemma. Quick honesty note: none actually supports the strong "focus almost exclusively on interesting work" claim—the evidence is more nuanced.

**Deci & Ryan (1985/2000) – Self-Determination Theory ("Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations")**
Intrinsic motivation (interest, autonomy, mastery) predicts persistence and well-being more robustly than external rewards. But it also identifies autonomy and competence as needs—leading a team could satisfy those even in "less interesting" work.

**Csikszentmihalyi (1990) – "Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience"**
Engagement and challenge-skill balance ("flow") are major drivers of life satisfaction, often outweighing pay. Note that flow comes from challenge fit, which a stretch role like team leadership can also provide.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010) – "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being"**
Higher income raises life evaluation but day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus (~$75k). Suggests a modest salary bump won't move daily happiness much—weakening the "go for money" side.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023) – "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved"**
Updated data shows well-being keeps rising with income for most people, except an unhappy minority. Implies money matters more than the 2010 paper claimed—so don't dismiss the raise entirely.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997) – "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
Whether people see work as a job, career, or calling predicts satisfaction more than the actual tasks. Your framing of each role may matter as much as the role itself.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) – "The Progress Principle"**
Daily sense of meaningful progress is the strongest driver of motivation and inner work life. Both "interesting research" and "leading a team toward goals" can supply this.

**Gilbert et al. – work on affective forecasting (e.g., "Stumbling on Happiness," 2006)**
People systematically mispredict how future choices will feel, overweighting salient differences. Caution: you may be overestimating how much "less interesting" will actually bother you long-term.

Bottom line the papers actually suggest: interest matters a lot, but so do autonomy, growth, and meaning—and leadership offers those too.

Which would help more?

Quick honest caveat: the strong version of your friends' claim—focus on "interesting" *almost exclusively*—isn't really what the literature supports. The research points to interest/intrinsic motivation being *important and often undervalued*, but alongside autonomy, relatedness, mastery, and yes, income. A few relevant papers:

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being."** Day-to-day emotional well-being rises with income up to ~$75k then plateaus, but overall life satisfaction keeps climbing. **Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year."** Challenges the plateau: both experienced and evaluative well-being continue rising with income. A counterweight to "ignore the salary."

**Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved."** Reconciliation: more money keeps improving happiness for most people, but plateaus for an already-unhappy minority. So salary's value depends partly on your baseline.

**Silvia (2008), "Interest—The Curious Emotion."** Interest is driven by novelty/complexity *and* by your sense of being able to cope with/understand something. Relevant because "interesting" isn't fixed—a manageable new challenge (like leading) can generate interest you don't anticipate.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS)**
Found that day-to-day emotional well-being stops rising around a threshold income while overall life satisfaction keeps climbing. Often cited to argue money matters less than people think above a certain point.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year" (PNAS)**
Using real-time data, found well-being continues rising with income with no plateau. A direct, important counterpoint to the "money stops mattering" narrative.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" (PNAS)**
An adversarial collaboration showing happiness keeps rising with income for most people, with a flattening only among an already-unhappy minority. The nuanced takeaway: money helps, but less so once other needs are met.

Argues intrinsic motivation and well-being depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness, not just rewards. Relevant because both "interesting work" and "leading a team" can satisfy these needs differently.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory"**
Identifies task significance, autonomy, skill variety, and feedback as core drivers of motivation and satisfaction. Useful framework to compare your two roles concretely rather than abstractly.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, careers, and callings"**
Distinguishes people who see work as a job, career, or calling, with the latter linked to higher satisfaction. Suggests *how* you frame the role matters as much as the role itself.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a job: Revisioning employees as active crafters of their work"**
Shows people can reshape a less-interesting role to make it more meaningful. Implies the Google job's "less interesting" label may be more malleable than it seems.

Bottom line your friends are half-right: interest is genuinely undervalued, but the evidence doesn't support ignoring salary, growth, and leadership goals.

Here are real, relevant papers. Note that they collectively support a *nuanced* version of your friends' claim, not the "almost exclusively" version.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS)**
Found that day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around a moderate income threshold, while life *evaluation* keeps rising with money. This is the classic basis for "money matters less than you think above a point."

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" (PNAS)**
A reconciliation showing experienced well-being generally keeps rising with income for most people, contradicting a hard plateau. This tempers the "interesting beats money" claim—higher salary does tend to help happiness.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Self-Determination Theory argues autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive durable motivation more than external rewards. Supports valuing intrinsically interesting work, but also flags that *leading a team* (autonomy/competence) is itself a strong intrinsic motivator.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory"**
The Job Characteristics Model identifies skill variety, task significance, and autonomy as predictors of satisfaction. Relevant because "interesting" is only one dimension—your desire to lead maps onto autonomy and growth.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a job: Revisioning employees as active crafters of their work"**
Shows people can reshape less-interesting roles to be more meaningful through "job crafting." Suggests the Google role's lower interest may be partly malleable rather than fixed.

**Ariely, Gneezy, Loewenstein & Mazar (2009), "Large stakes and big mistakes"**
Demonstrates that high financial incentives can sometimes reduce performance and motivation crowding effects. Cautions against treating the salary bump as a pure gain.

**Gilbert et al. / Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting"**
People systematically mispredict how much future circumstances (a raise, a new role) will affect their happiness. Directly relevant: both you and your friends are likely overweighting whichever factor you currently favor.

Bottom line the literature actually supports: interest matters and is underweighted by many people, but "almost exclusively" overstates it—autonomy, leadership growth, and income all independently predict well-being.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis," *Psychological Bulletin*.**
Finds intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially quality of work. Crucially, it concludes intrinsic and extrinsic factors *jointly* predict outcomes rather than one dominating—directly undercutting an "almost exclusively" framing.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being," *American Psychologist*.**
Argues well-being depends on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness, not interest alone. Notably, leading a team can boost autonomy and competence, so a "less interesting" role isn't automatically worse on these dimensions.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis," *Journal of Vocational Behavior*.**
Finds the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (~0.15). Supports discounting the salary bump—but says nothing that elevates "interesting" above other factors.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory," *Organizational Behavior and Human Performance*.**
Identifies task significance, skill variety, autonomy, and feedback—not just intrinsic interest—as drivers of motivation and satisfaction. Implies you should weigh the autonomy and skill-stretch of team leadership, not only how "interesting" the research is.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved," *PNAS*.**
Reconciles earlier findings: for most people higher income continues to raise well-being, with no flat plateau. Suggests don't dismiss the salary increase as irrelevant.

**Amabile (1996), *Creativity in Context* / "Componential" intrinsic-motivation work.**
Shows intrinsic motivation reliably enhances creativity and that controlling extrinsic pressures can undermine it. Relevant if your edge is creative research output—but it's about creativity specifically, not life or career satisfaction broadly.

Bottom line: the literature backs taking interest seriously, but "focus on it almost exclusively" overstates it.

rows.answer:
Here are real papers relevant to the "is interesting work underrated?" question. Note they collectively suggest balance rather than focusing on interest *almost exclusively*.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
Introduced the Job Characteristics Model, showing that task variety, significance, and autonomy drive intrinsic motivation and satisfaction. Job interest matters, but its effect is strongest for people high in "growth need strength," so it's not universal.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Self-Determination Theory argues autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive well-being more than external rewards like pay. Leading a team can satisfy competence/relatedness needs, complicating a pure "interest-only" view.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around ~$75k while life satisfaction keeps rising with income. Suggests salary matters for how you judge your life, even if not your moment-to-moment mood.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"**
Reconciled prior findings, showing well-being generally keeps rising with income for most people, with a plateau only for an already-unhappy minority. Undercuts the claim that money beyond a point is irrelevant.

**Csikszentmihalyi (1990/research program), "Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience"**
Documents that deep engagement ("flow") in challenging, intrinsically interesting tasks is a major source of life quality. Supports valuing interesting work, but flow depends on skill-challenge match, not just topic novelty.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Progress Principle"**
Shows that the single biggest daily motivator is making meaningful progress on work one cares about. Relevant because a "less interesting" role can still feel rewarding if it offers visible progress and growth (e.g., leadership).

Bottom line from the literature: interesting work is genuinely important and often underweighted relative to salary alone, but "almost exclusively" overstates it—pay, autonomy, mastery, and relationships all independently predict well-being.

rows.answer:
A few caveats before the list: I won't fabricate citations, so I'm only including work I'm fairly confident is real, and I'll flag that the strong claim ("focus almost exclusively") is *not* what this literature actually concludes. The research mostly says interest/meaning matters more than people predict—not that pay and growth are irrelevant.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being."** Argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive durable motivation and well-being more than external rewards. Relevant because "interesting work" maps onto intrinsic motivation, but note the theory equally emphasizes *competence and growth*—which the team-lead role could provide.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being."** Found life-evaluation rises with income but daily emotional well-being plateaus around ~$75k. Often cited to downplay salary, but it's about absolute income levels, not "interesting vs. boring work."

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year."** Using large real-time sampling, found well-being keeps rising with income well past the old plateau. This actually *complicates* the "money doesn't matter" half of your friends' argument.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved."** Reconciles the two papers above: more money helps most people, but for the already-unhappy the effect flattens. Implies money's value depends on your baseline—not a blanket case for ignoring salary.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work."** Shows people can reshape tasks and relationships to make a given role more meaningful. Relevant because a "less interesting" job can often be made more interesting—so the choice isn't as fixed as it seems.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work."** Distinguishes work orientations and links the "calling" orientation to higher satisfaction independent of pay or prestige. Supports your friends partially, but the data is correlational and doesn't say to ignore other factors.

Bottom line from the actual literature: interest/meaning is genuinely undervalued in predictions, *and* leadership/competence growth and income also robustly matter—so "almost exclusively" overstates it.

rows.answer:
Short answer: the "focus almost exclusively on interesting work" claim is an overstatement of a real but more nuanced literature. Here are actual papers worth knowing:

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS)**
Found that life evaluation keeps rising with income, but day-to-day emotional well-being plateaued around ~$75k. Often cited to argue money matters less than people think above a threshold.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year" (PNAS)**
Using real-time experience sampling, found both happiness and life satisfaction continue rising with income with no clear plateau. This complicates the "money doesn't buy happiness past a point" story your friends may be leaning on.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" (PNAS)**
Reconciled the above: for most people happiness rises with income, but there's an "unhappy minority" whose suffering isn't fixed by more money. Implies the income-vs-interest tradeoff depends heavily on your baseline emotional state.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality)**
Distinguishes work seen as a job (money), career (advancement), or calling (intrinsic meaning); calling-oriented people report higher satisfaction. Relevant, but note it's correlational and doesn't show you must maximize "interesting" over all else.

**Deci & Ryan — Self-Determination Theory (2000), "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits" (Psychological Inquiry)**
Argues well-being depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not just intrinsic interest. Notably, *leading a team* directly serves autonomy and relatedness, so the Meta role isn't simply the "less fulfilling" choice.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work" (Job Characteristics Model)**
Identifies five drivers of motivation: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. "Interesting" maps to only some of these; a management role can raise autonomy and significance even if intrinsic task interest drops.

**Gilbert et al. / hedonic adaptation literature (e.g., Frederick & Loewenstein, 1999, "Hedonic Adaptation")**
People adapt to both salary gains and novel interesting work faster than they predict. Suggests neither factor delivers as much lasting happiness as anticipated—weakening any "focus exclusively on X" argument.

Bottom line from the literature: intrinsic interest matters a lot, but "almost exclusively" isn't supported—autonomy, leadership/relatedness, and income all independently contribute.

**Deci & Ryan, "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions" (2000)**
Foundational work on Self-Determination Theory, arguing intrinsic motivation (doing things because they're inherently interesting/satisfying) supports persistence and well-being more than external rewards. It supports *valuing* interest—but treats autonomy and competence as equally central, not interest alone.

**Kahneman & Deaton, "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (2010, PNAS)**

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" (2023, PNAS)**
A collaborative reconciliation: more income generally helps well-being, except for an unhappy minority where the benefit flattens. The nuanced takeaway is that money helps but with diminishing and unevenly distributed returns—not that it's irrelevant.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, "Jobs, Careers, and Callings" (1997, Journal of Research in Personality)**
Distinguishes people who see work as a job, career, or calling, with "calling" orientations linked to higher satisfaction. Relevant to whether intrinsic engagement matters—but it's about *orientation*, not advice to maximize interestingness.

rows.answer:
A quick honest caveat first: the strong version of your friends' claim ("focus on interesting work *almost exclusively*") isn't really what the evidence supports. The literature suggests interest/meaning matters a lot *and* that pay, autonomy, and growth (like leading a team) matter too. Relevant work:

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis."**
Found a surprisingly weak correlation between pay *level* and job satisfaction. This is the strongest empirical backing for "money matters less than you think," though it's about level, not a raise away from a baseline.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory."**
The Job Characteristics Model shows task variety, significance, and autonomy drive intrinsic motivation and satisfaction. Supports prioritizing interesting work, but also flags autonomy/responsibility—which a leadership role provides.

**Deci & Ryan, Self-Determination Theory (e.g., 2000, "The 'what' and 'why' of goal pursuits").**
Argues well-being depends on autonomy, competence, and relatedness, not just intrinsic interest. Leading a team can satisfy competence and relatedness even if the work is "less interesting."

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved."**
Reconciled earlier conflicting findings, showing that for most people higher income continues to improve well-being. Tempers any claim that salary should be dismissed.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Progress Principle" (and related HBR work).**
Meaningful daily progress is the top driver of positive work life. Suggests "interesting" matters, but the key variable is *progress on meaningful work*, which a stretch role like leadership can also deliver.

Bottom line the papers point to: interest matters, but so does growth into a role you've always wanted—your friends are half-right, not "almost exclusively" right.

Here are real, relevant papers—though I'll flag upfront that the literature supports interesting work mattering *a lot*, not the "almost exclusively" framing your friends suggest.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
Establishes the Job Characteristics Model, showing that task variety, significance, and autonomy strongly predict satisfaction and intrinsic motivation. Notably, *autonomy* and *leading meaningful work* (relevant to your team-lead desire) are core dimensions too—not just "interestingness."

Self-Determination Theory argues well-being rests on autonomy, competence, and relatedness, with intrinsic interest being durable while external rewards can crowd it out. But it also stresses that managing/relatedness needs (people leadership) are genuine drivers, complicating a pure "interest-only" rule.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"**
Reconciles earlier findings: for most people happiness keeps rising with income, with no plateau, except for an already-unhappy minority. This directly undercuts "salary barely matters"—money's effect is real and ongoing.

Distinguishes day-to-day emotional well-being from overall life evaluation, finding income reliably boosts the latter. Suggests a higher-paid role can improve how you judge your life even if daily experience is similar.

**Csikszentmihalyi (1990), "Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience"**
Shows engagement peaks when challenge matches skill, which "interesting" work tends to provide—but flow can arise in *any* sufficiently challenging task, including new managerial challenges.

Bottom line the papers actually support: interest is underrated relative to salary, but autonomy, leadership/growth, and income all independently matter—so "focus on interest almost exclusively" overstates the evidence.
