rows.answer:
A few caveats first: the literature doesn't cleanly say "interesting" is underrated and should dominate. It points to *several* drivers (meaning, autonomy, competence, relatedness, progress, and pay), and the leadership role you've always wanted maps onto some of those too. Relevant papers:

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"** — Introduces the Job Characteristics Model, where task variety and intrinsic interest boost motivation and satisfaction. But it weights autonomy and felt impact just as heavily as the work being interesting.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** — Argues well-being rests on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Notably, leading a team can satisfy relatedness and competence even if the content is less interesting.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"** — Finds that whether people experience work as a "calling" predicts satisfaction independent of income or prestige. Suggests your subjective framing matters as much as the objective task.

**Csikszentmihalyi (1990), "Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience"** — Shows that absorbing, appropriately challenging tasks produce flow, which correlates with well-being. This is the strongest support for your friends' "interesting matters" claim.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Progress Principle"** — Identifies daily progress in meaningful work as the single biggest driver of positive "inner work life." Implies a sense of advancement and impact can rival raw intellectual interest.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"** — Higher pay reliably raises life-evaluation but has limited effect on day-to-day emotional well-being. Partially supports discounting the salary bump.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year"** — Counterevidence: well-being keeps climbing with income with no clear plateau. So don't dismiss the higher salary entirely.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** — Finds only a weak correlation between pay level and job satisfaction across studies. Supports the idea that the salary difference should be a minor factor.

Bottom line the research actually supports: interest is *one* important factor, but autonomy, leadership/relatedness, and a sense of progress are comparably weighted—so the "almost exclusively" part of your friends' claim is overstated.

rows.answer:
A few caveats before the list: real research supports that intrinsically interesting work matters a lot, but I'm not aware of any credible study saying to weight it *almost exclusively*—the evidence is more balanced, and your friends are likely overstating it. Here are the relevant papers:

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS)**
Found that how you *judge* your life keeps rising with income, but daily emotional well-being plateaus (famously around ~$75k). Often cited to argue money buys "life evaluation" more than moment-to-moment happiness.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year" (PNAS)**
Using real-time sampling, found both life satisfaction and experienced happiness continue rising with income, with no plateau. Suggests a higher salary can genuinely improve felt well-being.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" (PNAS)**
Reconciled the two findings: for most people happiness keeps rising with income, but an unhappy minority plateaus. Money helps unless your unhappiness stems from non-financial causes.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory"**
Their Job Characteristics Model shows skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback drive intrinsic motivation and satisfaction. Notably, "leading a team" can add autonomy and significance even if the technical work feels less interesting.

**Deci & Ryan — Self-Determination Theory (e.g., 2000, "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation")**
Argues autonomy, competence, and relatedness are the core drivers of durable motivation. Intrinsically interesting work sustains engagement better than external rewards alone.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Progress Principle" (and 2007 HBR work)**
Found that the single biggest daily motivator is making meaningful progress in work you care about. Implies "interesting" matters—but mainly when paired with a sense of progress and impact.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"**
Shows people can reshape boring roles into meaningful ones through "job crafting." Relevant because a less-interesting role isn't necessarily fixed in how interesting it feels.

Bottom line the literature actually supports: interest/meaning *and* income both matter, and autonomy from leading a team is its own well-being factor—so "almost exclusively interesting" isn't what the evidence shows.

rows.answer:
**Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000). "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions." *Contemporary Educational Psychology*.**
Foundational Self-Determination Theory paper arguing that intrinsically interesting, autonomy-satisfying work sustains motivation and well-being more durably than external rewards like pay. However, it stresses that competence and relatedness (e.g., leading a team) are *equally* core needs, not subordinate to "interest."

**Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010). "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis." *Journal of Vocational Behavior*.**
Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only weakly with job and pay satisfaction (~r = .15), suggesting more money buys surprisingly little lasting satisfaction. This supports your friends' point that chasing salary alone is overrated—but it says nothing about "interesting" specifically.

**Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010). "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being." *PNAS*.**
Found that day-to-day emotional well-being rises with income only up to a threshold, while life evaluation keeps climbing. Relevant because Meta's higher pay may improve how you *judge* your life even if not your daily mood.

**Killingsworth, M. A., Kahneman, D., & Mellers, B. (2023). "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved." *PNAS*.**
Reconciles earlier findings: for most people more income continues to raise happiness, with diminishing returns. This partly undercuts the "money barely matters" framing your friends imply.

**Wrzesniewski, A., & Dutton, J. E. (2001). "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work." *Academy of Management Review*.**
Shows that "how interesting" a job feels is partly malleable—people reshape tasks and meaning regardless of the role's objective content. Implies the "less interesting" Meta role might become interesting once you craft and lead it.

**Amabile, T. M., & Kramer, S. J. (2011). "The Power of Small Wins." *Harvard Business Review*.**
The "progress principle": meaningful progress in work is the strongest daily driver of motivation and positive emotion. Supports prioritizing engaging work—but defines meaning through *progress*, which a leadership role can amplify.

---

*Caveat: This literature does not cleanly establish that interesting work should be weighted "almost exclusively." It shows intrinsic interest matters a lot, money matters less than people assume but still matters, and that leadership/competence and the malleability of "interestingness" complicate the binary your friends suggest.*

rows.answer:
A note first: I won't invent citations, and the strong claim ("focus on interesting work almost exclusively") isn't really what this literature shows—it's more nuanced. Here are real, relevant papers:

**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation," *Psychological Bulletin*.**
Tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for inherently interesting tasks. Supports your friends' direction, but specifically about how pay can erode engagement, not a blanket rule.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory," *Organizational Behavior and Human Performance*.**
Task variety, autonomy, and significance drive satisfaction and performance more than many external factors. Relevant because "leading a team" adds autonomy/significance even if the core work is less interesting.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being," *PNAS*.**
Day-to-day emotional well-being stops rising with income past a threshold, though overall life evaluation keeps climbing. Suggests a salary bump matters less for daily happiness than the content of your days.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000/Year," *PNAS*.**
Found well-being continues rising with income, challenging the plateau idea. A useful counterweight—money isn't as easily dismissed as your friends imply.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved," *PNAS*.**
Reconciles the above: more money helps most people but does little for an already-unhappy minority. Implies the "right" choice depends on your baseline satisfaction, not a universal rule.

**Csikszentmihalyi (1990), *Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience*.**
Deep engagement ("flow") in challenging, interesting tasks is a major source of lasting fulfillment. Backs valuing interesting work—but note that a new leadership challenge can also produce flow.

**Gilbert et al. (1998), "Immune Neglect: A Source of Durability Bias in Affective Forecasting," *JPSP*.**
People systematically overestimate how long both good and bad outcomes will affect them. Relevant because you may be over-weighting how much "less interesting work" will actually bother you long-term.

Bottom line the papers actually suggest: interesting work matters a lot, but so do autonomy, leadership (a goal you've held), and income—your friends are partly right but overstating "almost exclusively."

rows.answer:
Quick caveat: the research is more nuanced than "focus almost exclusively on interesting work." Here are the relevant papers.

**On meaning/interest mattering:**

- **Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory."** Identifies skill variety, task significance, and autonomy as core drivers of intrinsic motivation and satisfaction. Suggests that the *content* of work strongly predicts engagement and well-being.

- **Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, careers, and callings: People's relations to their work."** People who view work as a "calling" report higher life and job satisfaction than those motivated by money or advancement. Supports the idea that finding work meaningful/interesting matters a lot.

- **Ariely, Kamenica & Prelec (2008), "Man's search for meaning: The case of Legos."** Experimentally shows people work harder and longer when their effort feels meaningful versus pointless. Even small losses of meaning sharply reduce motivation.

- **Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions."** Foundational Self-Determination Theory work showing intrinsic motivation (interest, autonomy, competence) sustains effort better than external rewards. External incentives can even crowd out intrinsic drive.

**On money mattering more than your friends suggest:**

- **Judge et al. (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis."** Pay level correlates only weakly with job satisfaction (~0.15). Backs the claim that more salary alone won't make you happier.

- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved."** For most people well-being keeps rising with income with no plateau, contradicting the older "$75k cap" finding. So money's contribution is real and ongoing, not negligible.

**On the leadership angle (which your friends ignore):**

- **Sutin et al. / and broadly the SDT literature on competence and growth** suggest pursuing a long-desired role (like leading a team) satisfies competence and growth needs, which independently predict well-being—meaning "interesting tasks" isn't the only intrinsic factor at play.

Bottom line the papers support: interest matters, but so do autonomy, growth, and meaningful new challenges (like leadership)—not interest "almost exclusively."

Below are real papers relevant to your question. Note up front: I couldn't find research supporting the strong claim that interesting work should be pursued "almost exclusively"—the actual literature suggests interest/meaning matters a lot but trades off against other factors, so your friends are partly right but overstating it.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
Identifies core job dimensions (skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, feedback) that drive motivation and satisfaction. "Interesting work" maps onto several of these, but autonomy and significance matter just as much as novelty.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation"**
Argues intrinsic motivation is sustained by autonomy, competence, and relatedness rather than interest alone. Leading a team (relatedness/competence) could satisfy these needs even if the work itself is less novel.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Found that day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around a moderate income while life satisfaction keeps rising with money. Suggests a higher salary helps how you evaluate your life more than how you feel daily.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"**
Reconciles earlier findings, showing money generally keeps improving well-being but with diminishing returns, except for an unhappy minority. Implies salary gains matter but won't offset chronic dissatisfaction with the work.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (and *The Progress Principle*)**
Shows that perceiving meaningful progress in one's work is the single strongest daily driver of motivation and emotion. Reinforces that engaging, meaningful work is genuinely high-value—but "progress" can come from leadership challenges too.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
Distinguishes whether people frame work as a job, career, or calling, which strongly predicts satisfaction independent of the actual role. Your own framing of either offer may matter more than the objective "interestingness."

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Lays out Self-Determination Theory, arguing that intrinsic interest, autonomy, and competence drive durable engagement better than external rewards. However, it stresses that extrinsic factors (like pay) aren't irrelevant—they matter when they support rather than undermine autonomy.

Distinguishes people who see work as a job (for pay), a career (for advancement), or a calling (intrinsically fulfilling), finding "calling" orientation predicts higher satisfaction. It suggests how you frame the work matters as much as the work itself, which complicates the "less interesting = worse" assumption.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
Introduces the Job Characteristics Model: skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict motivation and satisfaction. Notably, autonomy and responsibility (relevant to leading a team) are core drivers—not just intrinsic "interestingness."

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-being"**
Found that life evaluation keeps rising with income, but day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around a threshold. This is the study often (mis)cited to argue money stops mattering past a point.

**Ariely, Kamenica & Prelec (2008), "Man's Search for Meaning: The Case of Legos"**
Experimentally shows people work harder and longer when their output feels meaningful, and effort collapses when work feels pointless. Supports the value of meaning—but it's about *meaning/significance*, not "interestingness" per se.

**Csikszentmihalyi (1990), "Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience"**
Argues peak engagement ("flow") comes from challenging, skill-stretching tasks—often correlating with interesting work. But flow depends on challenge-skill balance, which a new leadership role could also provide.

Bottom line your friends are overstating it: interest is genuinely important and arguably underrated relative to salary, but the evidence treats autonomy, meaning, leadership/growth, and income as multiple real factors—not a single one to optimize exclusively.

Here are real, relevant papers — though note up front that none support the "almost exclusively" framing your friends used; the literature is more nuanced (and includes findings that cut against them).

**Herzberg, F. (1968). "One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?" *Harvard Business Review*.**
Argues that interesting work, achievement, and responsibility are true "motivators," while salary is only a "hygiene factor" that prevents dissatisfaction without creating satisfaction. This is the strongest classic support for your friends' view.

**Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000). "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits." *Psychological Inquiry*.**
Self-Determination Theory holds that intrinsic motivation rests on autonomy, competence, and relatedness, not just task interest. Relevant because leading a team can boost autonomy and relatedness even if the work is "less interesting."

**Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010). "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being." *PNAS*.**
Found that day-to-day emotional well-being stops rising past roughly $75k/year, while overall life evaluation keeps climbing. Suggests a higher salary may help how you judge your life more than how you feel daily.

**Killingsworth, M. (2021). "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year." *PNAS*.**
Challenges the plateau, finding well-being continues increasing with income for most people. Important counterweight to "money doesn't matter" arguments.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers (2023). "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved." *PNAS*.**
Reconciles the above: income generally keeps improving well-being for most people, with a flatter effect only for an already-unhappy minority. Undercuts any claim that pay is irrelevant once "comfortable."

**Amabile, T., & Kramer, S. (2011). "The Power of Small Wins." *Harvard Business Review* (and *The Progress Principle*).**
Identifies making meaningful progress as the single most powerful driver of positive inner work life. Suggests "interesting" matters less than feeling you're advancing on work that matters to you.

**Wrzesniewski, A., et al. (1997). "Jobs, Careers, and Callings." *Journal of Research in Personality*.**
Distinguishes how people relate to work and links a "calling" orientation to higher satisfaction. Relevant for weighing intrinsic interest against status/advancement (the management role).

Honest bottom line: the evidence supports interesting/meaningful work being *important and often underweighted* — but not focusing on it "almost exclusively," especially given that autonomy, leadership, and income also robustly predict well-being.

rows.answer:
**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved," PNAS.** Reconciles earlier findings to show that higher income generally keeps improving well-being for most people, undercutting the idea that pay should be dismissed once "enough." The effect is real but modest, so salary matters but rarely dominates.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work," Journal of Research in Personality.** People who view work as a "calling" (intrinsically engaging and meaningful) report higher life and job satisfaction than those motivated mainly by money or advancement. Supports your friends' intuition that engagement matters a lot.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation," American Psychologist.** Sustained motivation and well-being depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not just interesting tasks. Notably, "leading a team" can satisfy competence and relatedness needs that pure individual research does not.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins," Harvard Business Review.** The single biggest daily driver of motivation is making meaningful progress in work you care about. Suggests "interesting" matters specifically because it fuels a sense of progress.

**Gilbert & Wilson (2007), "Prospective Affective Forecasting," / Gilbert's affective-forecasting research.** People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, often overweighting salient features and adapting faster than expected. Cautions that your current judgment of which job is "more interesting" may be unreliable.

**Newport, "So Good They Can't Help It" (2012) (synthesizing passion/mastery research).** Argues the "follow your passion" framing is overrated; mastery, autonomy, and impact often *generate* interest rather than precede it. Directly challenges the claim that one should optimize for "interesting" almost exclusively.

Net: the literature supports that engagement matters greatly, but not the "almost exclusively" part—autonomy, growth (e.g., leadership), and income all contribute, and your forecasts about both jobs are likely biased.

rows.answer:
A quick honest caveat: the literature is more nuanced than "focus on interesting almost exclusively"—it suggests *interest/intrinsic motivation matters a lot*, but money, status, and leadership growth also independently predict well-being. Relevant papers:

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being."**
Argues that satisfying autonomy, competence, and relatedness drives durable motivation and well-being more than external rewards. Notably, *competence and autonomy* (which your leadership role offers) are core needs too—not just interest.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory."**
Identifies "skill variety," "task significance," and "experienced meaningfulness" as key job features predicting satisfaction. Interesting work scores high here, but so does a role with greater scope and responsibility.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being."**
Found life satisfaction keeps rising with income while day-to-day mood plateaued (~$75k). Often cited to downplay money—but it's been substantially revised.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved."**
Shows that for most people emotional well-being *keeps rising* with income, contradicting the earlier plateau. So the "money barely matters" intuition is weaker than commonly claimed.

**Csikszentmihalyi (1990), "Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience."**
Engagement/flow from challenging, intrinsically interesting tasks is a major source of satisfaction. Supports your friends—but flow can also arise in newly challenging roles like managing a team.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work."**
Shows people can reshape "less interesting" jobs to increase meaning and engagement. Implies the Meta role's "less interesting" framing may be partly malleable.

**Ariely, Kamenica & Prelec (2008), "Man's Search for Meaning: The Case of Legos."**
Demonstrates that perceived meaning/purpose strongly affects willingness to work, beyond pay. Supports that interest/meaning is underrated—but doesn't say to ignore other factors.

Bottom line from the evidence: interest is real and undervalued, *and* so are autonomy, competence, leadership growth, and income—your choice is genuinely a multi-factor trade-off, not a single-variable one.

rows.answer:
A few genuinely relevant papers (with a caveat: none support focusing on interest "almost exclusively" — they show it's *one major factor among several*, including salary, autonomy, and team relationships):

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Intrinsic interest in a task is a powerful and durable driver of motivation and well-being, often outweighing external rewards. But the same theory ranks *autonomy* and *relatedness* (e.g., leading a team) as equally fundamental needs—so this cuts both ways for your decision.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS)**
Higher income reliably improves how people *evaluate* their lives overall, with diminishing emotional returns past a threshold. This suggests salary isn't irrelevant, but its day-to-day happiness impact is limited.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000/year" (PNAS)**
Contrary to the older threshold idea, well-being keeps rising with income for most people. This weakens the "money barely matters, just chase interesting work" framing your friends are using.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Progress Principle" (and related HBR work)**
The single biggest day-to-day motivator at work is making meaningful progress, not novelty or interest per se. "Interesting" and "meaningful" aren't the same thing—a leadership role can supply meaning even if the technical work is less stimulating.

**Csikszentmihalyi, "Flow" research (1990 onward)**
Engagement/flow comes from challenge matched to skill, which is related to but distinct from a topic being "interesting." A managerial role offering a new skill challenge could generate flow even on less intrinsically interesting subject matter.

Honest bottom line: the research supports interest as important, but the "almost exclusively" claim is an overstatement of what these studies actually find.

rows.answer:
**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
Identifies five core job dimensions—skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback—that predict satisfaction and motivation. "Interesting work" maps onto variety/significance, but autonomy and feedback matter just as much, suggesting interest alone is incomplete.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Argues intrinsic motivation (genuine interest) produces more durable engagement and well-being than external rewards like pay. However, it stresses *competence and autonomy* as co-requirements, so leadership opportunity could satisfy these even in "less interesting" work.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around a moderate income while life satisfaction keeps rising with money. Implies the salary bump may boost how you *evaluate* your life more than how you *feel* daily.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"**
Shows higher income generally continues improving well-being, except for an unhappy minority where money doesn't help. Suggests salary matters more than the 2010 plateau implied, weakening a pure "ignore the pay" stance.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"**
Demonstrates people systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, often overestimating impact and duration. Your friends' (and your own) confidence about what will feel "interesting" or satisfying may be unreliable.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
Finds that whether work feels meaningful depends heavily on personal orientation, not just objective task content. A "less interesting" role can become engaging through how you frame and shape it (job crafting).

---

Net read: research supports interest/intrinsic motivation as important, but does **not** justify weighing it "almost exclusively"—autonomy, leadership growth, income, and your poor ability to forecast future satisfaction all genuinely count.

rows.answer:
A quick honest caveat first: no credible study supports focusing on interesting work "almost exclusively"—the evidence shows interest/meaning matters a lot but coexists with income, autonomy, and relationships. Relevant reading:

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis"**
Pay level correlates only weakly with job and pay satisfaction (r ≈ .15). This supports your friends' direction: more money buys surprisingly little lasting satisfaction once basic needs are met.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory" (Job Characteristics Model)**
Intrinsic motivation and satisfaction rise with skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback—not pay. Note that "leading a team" can raise autonomy and significance, partially offsetting "less interesting" content.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory: Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations"**
Well-being is driven by autonomy, competence, and relatedness rather than external rewards. A leadership role can satisfy competence/relatedness needs even if the technical work is duller.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Progress Principle" / "The Power of Small Wins"**
The single biggest daily booster of motivation is making progress on meaningful work. Argues the *content* you find engaging matters more than perks—but "meaningful" includes mastering new challenges like management.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being"**
Day-to-day emotional well-being stopped rising past ~$75k/year. Suggests a salary bump may not move your daily happiness much.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved"**
For most people happiness keeps rising with income, but the slope is shallow and flat for the already-satisfied. Tempers the "money doesn't matter" reading your friends imply.

**Bailey & Madden (2016), "What Makes Work Meaningful—Or Meaningless" (MIT Sloan)**
Meaningfulness is episodic and tied to feeling your work matters, not to constant excitement. Implies you should weigh whether the Meta role offers meaning (team impact) beyond the work being "interesting."

**Sherman et al. (2012), "Leadership is associated with lower levels of stress"**
Higher leadership rank was linked to lower cortisol and anxiety, attributed to greater sense of control. Relevant to your long-held wish to lead—management may improve well-being via control even if the work is less novel.

Bottom line from the literature: interest/meaning is genuinely underrated *relative to salary*, so your friends are partly right—but autonomy, mastery, and leadership (which you want) are independent contributors, so "almost exclusively interesting work" isn't what the research says.

**Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000). "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions."** Establishes self-determination theory, showing that intrinsically interesting work sustains motivation and well-being better than external rewards. However, it stresses autonomy and competence too—not interest alone—and warns that extrinsic factors still matter when basic needs are unmet.

**Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976). "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory."** The Job Characteristics Model finds that task variety, significance, and autonomy drive satisfaction and performance. Notably, skill variety and autonomy (relevant to a leadership role) are also core dimensions, so "interesting" isn't the only psychological lever.

**Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010). "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being."** Found that day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around a moderate income while life evaluation keeps rising with money. This nuances the "interest over money" claim: higher pay still improves how you judge your life overall.

**Killingsworth, M. A., Kahneman, D., & Mellers, B. (2023). "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved."** Shows well-being generally keeps rising with income for most people, with a plateau mainly for an already-unhappy minority. Undercuts any strong claim that salary is irrelevant once "interesting work" is secured.

**Wrzesniewski, A., & Dutton, J. E. (2001). "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work."** Demonstrates that workers can reshape tasks and relationships to find meaning even in less inherently interesting roles. Implies a "less interesting" job can be made more engaging rather than treated as fixed.

**Amabile, T., & Kramer, S. (2011). "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)."** Finds that the single biggest daily motivator is making progress on meaningful work. Leading a team can supply this through enabling others' progress, even if the technical content is less novel.

One honest caveat your friends omit: this literature supports interest as *underweighted*, not as something to optimize "almost exclusively"—pay, autonomy, and the rare chance to lead all carry real, measurable weight.

A few caveats before the list: these are real, well-known works, but the claim that one should focus on "interesting work" *almost exclusively* overstates what the research actually supports. Most of it says interesting/meaningful work matters a lot—often more than people predict—but alongside autonomy, progress, and a baseline of fair pay, not instead of them.

**Herzberg, "One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?" (HBR, 1968/2003)**
Argues that "motivators" like interesting work, achievement, and growth drive real satisfaction, while pay is a "hygiene factor" that mainly prevents dissatisfaction. Supports your friends—but note pay below expectations actively demotivates.

**Ryan & Deci, "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations" / Self-Determination Theory (2000)**
Intrinsic motivation (doing work because it's inherently engaging) predicts better performance and well-being than external rewards. Importantly, autonomy *and* competence both matter—your desired leadership role could feed competence/growth needs.

**Csikszentmihalyi, "Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience" (1990)**
The most rewarding states come from absorbing, appropriately challenging tasks—often work, not leisure. Loss of intellectually engaging "flow" work is a real cost worth weighing.

**Amabile & Kramer, "The Progress Principle" (2011)**
Analysis of thousands of daily diaries found that making progress in meaningful work is the single largest day-to-day driver of motivation. Suggests "interesting" matters, but so does feeling you're making meaningful progress—possible in a leadership role too.

**Kahneman & Deaton, "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS, 2010)**
Above a moderate income, more money raises life-satisfaction judgments but does little for daily emotional well-being. Tempers the appeal of the higher Meta salary.

**Killingsworth, "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000/year" (PNAS, 2021)**
A large study found well-being keeps rising with income, contradicting a strict "money stops mattering" threshold. A useful counterweight—don't fully dismiss the raise.

**Wrzesniewski et al., "Jobs, Careers, and Callings" (1997)**
People who see work as a "calling" report higher satisfaction than those who see it as a job or career. Relevant if the Google work feels closer to a calling.

Bottom line the literature actually supports: interesting work is *underrated relative to salary*, but not to the point of ignoring growth (leadership), autonomy, and fair pay entirely.

A quick honest caveat: there's no single study showing "interestingness is underrated and should be pursued almost exclusively"—that's an overstatement of a real but more nuanced literature. Here are the genuinely relevant papers/works:

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Finds that well-being depends heavily on autonomy, competence, and relatedness rather than external rewards alone. Intrinsically interesting work satisfies these needs, but so can leading a team (relatedness, competence)—so it cuts both ways.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work"**
Job satisfaction rises with skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback—not just interest per se. A "less interesting" management role can still score high if it offers autonomy and meaning.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
Shows that satisfaction depends less on the objective job than on whether you frame it as a calling. How you construe the Meta role may matter as much as its content.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
A higher salary reliably boosts life-evaluation but has limited effect on day-to-day emotional well-being past a threshold. Supports discounting the raise somewhat—but doesn't say to ignore it.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"**
Income generally keeps improving well-being, except for an already-unhappy minority. Suggests your friends overstate the "money doesn't matter" case.

**Gilbert & Wilson (2007), "Prospection: Experiencing the Future" (and Gilbert's affective-forecasting work)**
People systematically mispredict what will make them happy and adapt to changes faster than expected. Implies you may overestimate both the joy of "interesting" and the pain of "less interesting."

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" / *The Progress Principle***
The single biggest daily motivator is making meaningful progress, which interesting work enables. But leading a team toward shared progress is itself a strong, distinct source of meaning.

Bottom line the literature supports: interest/intrinsic motivation is undervalued relative to salary, but leadership, autonomy, and meaning are also powerful—so "focus on interesting almost exclusively" isn't what the research says.

Finds that the single biggest daily motivator is making meaningful progress, which interesting work enables. Yet "meaningful" was defined broadly, including helping others—something managing a team directly provides.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory."** Proposes that intrinsically meaningful work depends on five dimensions—skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback—not "interestingness" alone. Notably, autonomy and significance (which a leadership role can boost) drive satisfaction as much as inherent task interest.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions" (Self-Determination Theory).** Argues well-being and sustained motivation rest on three needs: competence, autonomy, and *relatedness*. Leading a team strongly serves relatedness and competence, so "interesting" is only one input among several, undercutting an exclusive focus.

**Herzberg (1968), "One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?" (Two-Factor Theory).** Distinguishes "motivators" (achievement, growth, responsibility) from "hygiene factors" (salary, conditions), claiming interest-type factors drive satisfaction while pay mainly prevents dissatisfaction. This partly supports your friends, but also flags that responsibility/leadership are core motivators, not just task content.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work."** Finds satisfaction depends more on how people frame work (especially as a "calling") than on the objective job, and that meaning can be actively crafted. Implies interest is partly malleable, weakening the case for choosing solely on current "interestingness."

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-being."** Money raises life satisfaction broadly but stops boosting day-to-day emotional well-being above a threshold. Suggests the salary bump matters less than the non-monetary trade-offs you're weighing.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000/year."** Challenges the threshold view, showing income keeps improving well-being at high levels. Relevant because it cautions against dismissing the higher OpenAI salary as irrelevant.

**Csikszentmihalyi (1990), "Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience."** Shows engagement peaks when skill meets challenge, not from "interesting content" per se. A stretch leadership role can generate flow even if the technical work feels less novel.

*Honest caveat: none of these support focusing on "interesting" almost exclusively—they collectively show autonomy, growth, leadership, meaning, and income all contribute, so your friends are overstating a real but partial finding.*

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**

**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation"**
Found that tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. This supports valuing interest, but speaks to *reward structure*, not to ignoring salary or career advancement.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Found income raises life-satisfaction judgments continuously, while day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around a threshold. Implies the higher salary helps your overall life evaluation but won't fix daily dissatisfaction if the work bores you.

Distinguished people who see work as a job (pay), career (advancement), or calling (intrinsic meaning), finding "calling" orientation predicts higher satisfaction. Suggests how *you* frame work matters as much as the role itself.

rows.answer:
Here are real, relevant papers (with honest caveats baked in):

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being," PNAS.** Found that life evaluation rises steadily with income, but day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around ~$75k/year. Suggests salary differences past a certain point matter less for everyday happiness than for how you judge your life overall.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year," PNAS.** Using real-time sampling, found no plateau—well-being kept rising with income. Directly challenges the simple "money stops mattering" narrative.

**Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved," PNAS.** An adversarial collaboration reconciling the two above: income keeps improving happiness for most people, but plateaus for an already-unhappy minority. The honest takeaway is "it depends on you," not a clean rule.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis," Journal of Vocational Behavior.** Across ~115 studies, pay level correlated only weakly with job satisfaction (~.15). Implies what you earn predicts your satisfaction surprisingly little.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory," Org. Behavior & Human Performance.** Introduced the Job Characteristics Model: skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback drive intrinsic motivation. Relevant because "leading a team" adds autonomy/significance even if the content is less interesting.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions," Contemporary Educational Psychology.** Self-Determination Theory argues sustained motivation comes from autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not just interest. Notably, *leadership* can satisfy competence/relatedness needs that pure "interesting work" does not.

**Herzberg (1968), "One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?," Harvard Business Review.** Two-factor theory: "hygiene" factors like pay prevent dissatisfaction but don't create satisfaction, while "motivators" (achievement, growth, the work itself) do. Partly supports your friends—but counts *growth/responsibility*, not just interestingness.

**A caveat your friends' claim glosses over:** none of these establish that "interesting" should be optimized "almost exclusively"—the literature points to a *bundle* (autonomy, growth, meaning, relationships), and leading a team can deliver several of those even if the topic excites you less.

rows.answer:
Here are real, relevant papers. A quick caveat first: the literature supports that interesting/meaningful work matters a lot, but *not* that it should be pursued "almost exclusively" — autonomy, mastery, and leadership opportunities (which your OpenAI offer has) are also strong, independent drivers of well-being.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work," *Organizational Behavior and Human Performance*.**
Introduces the Job Characteristics Model, showing that task significance, variety, and especially autonomy and feedback predict intrinsic motivation and satisfaction. "Interesting" work is one input among several, not the sole determinant.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations," *Contemporary Educational Psychology*.**
Self-Determination Theory argues that competence, autonomy, and relatedness drive lasting motivation, and that intrinsic interest is powerful but undermined when external rewards dominate. Relevant because leading a team can satisfy competence/relatedness even if the work is less inherently "interesting."

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings," *Journal of Research in Personality*.**
Distinguishes people who see work as a job (income), career (advancement), or calling (intrinsic fulfillment), finding "calling" orientation predicts higher life and job satisfaction. Suggests how *you frame* the role may matter as much as the role's objective interestingness.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being," *PNAS*.**
Money raises life-evaluation but has diminishing effects on day-to-day emotional well-being past a threshold. Supports your friends' point that a salary bump alone is a weak source of lasting happiness.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000/year," *PNAS*.**
Challenges the Kahneman plateau, finding well-being keeps rising with income for most people. Worth reading alongside the above as a corrective — money's role isn't as negligible as often claimed.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins," *Harvard Business Review*.**
Their "progress principle" shows that meaningful, visible progress in work is the single strongest daily driver of motivation and positive emotion. Implies a less "interesting" role can still energize you if it offers clear progress and impact.

**Judge et al. (2002), "Personality and Leadership," *Journal of Applied Psychology*.**
Finds leadership roles and effectiveness link to satisfaction and identity fulfillment for those motivated toward them. Relevant since you say leading a team is something you've always wanted — that desire is itself a meaningful signal.

Quick caveat: the research leans toward intrinsic interest mattering *a lot*, but "focus on it almost exclusively" overstates it—autonomy, leadership/growth, and income all interact. The most relevant work:

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory."**
Introduced the Job Characteristics Model, showing that task variety, significance, and autonomy drive intrinsic motivation and satisfaction. "Interesting" work maps onto these dimensions—but so does the autonomy a leadership role can provide.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being."**
Argues that competence, autonomy, and relatedness underlie durable motivation, and that external rewards can crowd out intrinsic interest. Supports your friends partly, but note leading a team can boost autonomy and relatedness even if the work is less stimulating.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being."**
Found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaued around ~$75k while life-evaluation kept rising. Suggests modest salary bumps add less happiness than people expect.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year."**
Using real-time sampling, found well-being keeps rising with income with no clear plateau. Complicates the "money doesn't matter" reading.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved."**
Reconciled the above: income generally raises well-being, except for an unhappy minority where the effect flattens. Bottom line—salary matters, but with diminishing returns.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisiting Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work."**
Shows people can reshape tasks and relationships to make work more meaningful. Relevant because a "less interesting" OpenAI role might be craftable, especially as a team lead.

**Amabile (1996), "Creativity in Context" / componential theory of creativity.**
Demonstrates intrinsic motivation is a key predictor of creative output. If creative research output is your goal, the more interesting role likely wins.

rows.answer:
**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" (PNAS)**
Higher income generally keeps improving well-being beyond old "plateau" estimates, but the effect is modest and flattens for most people. Salary matters, yet small raises rarely move the needle much unless you're currently unhappy.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS)**
Income strongly predicts how you *judge* your life but weakly predicts day-to-day emotional experience. A higher OpenAI offer may improve your life-evaluation more than your daily mood.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation" (American Psychologist)**
Sustained motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not just interest. "Interesting work" is really a proxy for competence/autonomy, and leadership can satisfy relatedness and autonomy too.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work" (OBHP)**
Job satisfaction comes from five features—skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback—of which "interesting" captures only a couple. Leading a team adds significance and autonomy, complicating any single-factor advice.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings" (Journal of Research in Personality)**
Whether work feels like a job, career, or calling depends more on personal orientation than the role itself. The same position can be experienced as boring or meaningful depending on framing.

**Amabile (1996/1983), "The Social Psychology of Creativity" / Intrinsic Motivation Principle of Creativity**
Intrinsic interest robustly boosts creative output and engagement in research-type work. This supports your friends *partially*—for creative roles, interest predicts performance, but it doesn't say to ignore everything else.

---

**Herzberg, F. (1968). "One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?" — *Harvard Business Review.*** Argues that "motivators" like interesting, challenging work and achievement drive real satisfaction, while pay and conditions are mere "hygiene" factors that only prevent dissatisfaction. This is the closest mainstream support for your friends' claim, but it's a decades-old framework with mixed empirical replication.

**Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976). "Motivation through the Design of Work." — *Organizational Behavior and Human Performance.*** The Job Characteristics Model shows that task variety, significance, and especially autonomy strongly predict motivation and satisfaction. Notably, *autonomy and leadership scope* (your OpenAI offer) are among the strongest factors, suggesting "interesting" isn't the only thing that matters.

**Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000). "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation." — *American Psychologist.*** Intrinsic interest matters, but so do competence and relatedness, and external rewards can sometimes undermine intrinsic motivation. This supports valuing interest—while cautioning that a higher salary won't necessarily compensate for lost engagement.

**Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010). "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being." — *PNAS.*** Found that day-to-day emotional well-being rises with income only up to a point, after which more money adds little. This is often cited to argue that salary differences matter less than the nature of the work.

**Killingsworth, M., Kahneman, D., & Mellers, B. (2023). "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved." — *PNAS.*** Reconciles the debate, showing income generally *does* keep improving well-being for most people, contradicting the strong "money doesn't matter" reading. This weakens the case for ignoring the OpenAI raise outright.

**Amabile, T., & Kramer, S. (2011). *The Progress Principle.*** Demonstrates that the single biggest daily motivator is making meaningful progress in work you care about. Supports prioritizing interesting work—but defines "meaning" via progress and impact, not novelty alone.

A caveat: no rigorous study supports the strong claim that interest should be weighted "almost exclusively." The literature suggests interest, autonomy/leadership, and compensation all matter, so your friends are partly right but overstating it.

Here are relevant real papers. Note that none of them support an "almost exclusively focus on interesting work" conclusion—they paint a more balanced picture, which I'd weigh against your friends' framing.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Self-Determination Theory shows intrinsic interest is powerful, but it's only one of three needs—autonomy and *competence/relatedness* (e.g., leading a team) matter just as much. Optimal motivation comes from satisfying all three, not maximizing interest alone.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
The Job Characteristics Model identifies five drivers of work motivation: skill variety, task identity, *task significance*, autonomy, and feedback. "Interestingness" maps to only part of this—autonomy and significance (often higher in leadership roles) are independently important.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People relate to work as a job, career, or calling, and life satisfaction tracks the calling orientation more than the objective work itself. This suggests how you *frame* a role matters as much as its inherent content.

**Kahneman & Killingsworth (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved"**
Higher income generally continues improving well-being beyond old thresholds, especially for already-happy people. So the salary bump isn't trivially dismissed as your friends imply.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Progress Principle"**
The strongest daily booster of "inner work life" is making meaningful progress, not novelty or interest per se. A leadership role enabling visible progress and impact can rival intrinsically "interesting" tasks.

**Judge et al. (2002), "Personality and Leadership: A Qualitative and Quantitative Review"**
For people with leadership motivation, attaining leadership roles is strongly tied to satisfaction and engagement. If leading a team is a long-held goal, that fit effect is a genuine, evidence-backed factor—not a distraction from "interesting work."

Bottom line from the literature: interest matters, but it's one of several roughly co-equal drivers (autonomy, competence, significance, fit, income), so "focus on it almost exclusively" overstates the evidence.

rows.answer:
A few caveats: the research is more nuanced than "focus almost exclusively on interesting," and some of these findings cut against your friends' claim. Relevant work:

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS)**
Day-to-day emotional well-being rose with income only up to ~$75k, after which more money didn't add much. This is often cited to argue that beyond a threshold, non-monetary factors like interest matter more.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000/year" (PNAS)**
Using real-time sampling, found well-being kept rising with income with no plateau, complicating the simple "money doesn't matter" story. Suggests dismissing the salary bump entirely would be a mistake.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" (PNAS)**
Reconciled the above: more money helps most people but does little for an already-unhappy minority. Implies your baseline satisfaction with the work itself conditions whether the raise will help.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation" (American Psychologist)**
Intrinsic motivation, autonomy, and competence strongly predict sustained engagement and well-being. Supports weighting "interesting," but also flags that autonomy/mastery (which leading a team can provide) matters too.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" / The Progress Principle (Harvard Business Review)**
Meaningful progress in work is the single biggest daily motivator. Favors interesting work—but note "meaningful" can come from leading and developing a team, not only from technical novelty.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings" (Journal of Research in Personality)**
People who view work as a "calling" report higher satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Partially supports your friends, though it shows orientation can be cultivated in many roles.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), Job Characteristics Model (Org. Behavior & Human Performance)**
Motivation depends on skill variety, task identity, significance, autonomy, and feedback—not interest alone. The management role may score high on autonomy/significance even if low on technical "interest."

Bottom line the papers actually support: interest matters a lot, but "almost exclusively" overstates it—your friends are partly right.

rows.answer:
Quick caveat: the "focus almost exclusively on interesting work" claim is an oversimplification—the literature points to *several* roughly co-equal drivers (autonomy, meaning, competence, relatedness, growth), not interest alone. Relevant work:

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations" / Self-Determination Theory**
Well-being and durable motivation depend on three needs—autonomy, competence, and *relatedness*—not interest alone. Leading a team can strongly satisfy relatedness and competence, which "interesting" solo work may not.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work"**
Identifies five job features driving motivation: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. "Interest" maps to only one or two of these, so trading them off against autonomy (leadership) is not obviously a loss.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings"**
People who frame work as a "calling" report the highest satisfaction—but this stems from perceived *meaning and contribution*, not novelty or intellectual interest per se. Suggests how you frame the OpenAI role may matter as much as its content.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Progress Principle"**
The single strongest day-to-day booster of "inner work life" is making meaningful progress, not task novelty. A leadership role enabling a team's progress can score highly here.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being"**
Higher income raises life *evaluation* but emotional well-being plateaus (~$75k, older data). Implies the salary bump has real but bounded value.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000"**
Challenges the plateau finding—well-being kept rising with income in newer data. The salary difference may matter more than the Kahneman result suggests.

**Judge et al. (2001), "The job satisfaction–job performance relationship: A qualitative and quantitative review"**
Satisfaction and performance are correlated but modestly (~.30), and causation is bidirectional. Don't assume "more interesting" automatically yields better outcomes or vice versa.

Bottom line from the literature: interest is *one* underrated factor, but autonomy, meaning, growth, and social relatedness (all plausibly higher in the leadership role) are weighted comparably—so "almost exclusively" overstates it.

rows.answer:
Here are real, relevant papers. A quick caveat first: I'm not aware of research framed exactly as "interesting work is underrated," so your friends are likely paraphrasing the broader intrinsic-motivation/meaning literature—which is suggestive but doesn't license focusing on "interesting" *almost exclusively*.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Foundational statement of Self-Determination Theory, arguing that autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive durable motivation. Notably, *leading a team* can satisfy competence/relatedness needs even if the task itself is less "interesting."

**Herzberg (1968), "One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?"**
Proposes that pay is mostly a "hygiene factor" that prevents dissatisfaction but doesn't create lasting satisfaction, while the work content itself does. Supports the intuition that higher salary alone won't sustain happiness.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being"**
Found day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around ~$75k while life-evaluation keeps rising with income. Often cited to argue money matters less than meaning—but see the correction below.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved"**
Reconciliation showing income generally keeps improving well-being for most people, with a plateau mainly for an already-unhappy minority. This tempers the "money doesn't matter" reading of the 2010 paper.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)**
Shows that the single biggest booster of inner work life is making meaningful progress, not novelty per se. "Interesting" matters less than *feeling effective at meaningful work*—which a leadership role could provide.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
Distinguishes how people frame work and shows satisfaction depends more on orientation/meaning than objective job features. Implies you can sometimes "craft" interest into a role rather than needing it pre-supplied.

Bottom line from the evidence: interest is real and underweighted in naïve salary-only thinking, but autonomy, mastery, progress, and meaning matter at least as much—so a leadership role you've long wanted isn't obviously the wrong choice.

A few caveats before the list: the research supports that intrinsic interest matters *a lot*, but the claim that one should focus on it "almost exclusively" is an overstatement—the same literature shows autonomy, growth, relationships, and (up to a point) money also drive well-being. Leading a team, which you said you've always wanted, actually maps onto several of these factors.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (*American Psychologist*)**
Argues that intrinsic motivation and well-being depend on satisfying three needs—autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not just interest alone. A leadership role can boost competence and relatedness even if the core task is less inherently interesting.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (*Organizational Behavior and Human Performance*)**
The Job Characteristics Model identifies five drivers of motivation: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. "Interesting work" is only one input; autonomy and significance (often higher in leadership) matter comparably.

**Amabile (1993), "Motivational Synergy: Toward New Conceptualizations of Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivation in the Workplace" (*Human Resource Management Review*)**
Shows intrinsic motivation strongly predicts creativity and performance, but extrinsic factors can complement rather than crowd it out. Suggests it's not an either/or between interesting work and salary.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (*PNAS*)**
Found life satisfaction keeps rising with income while day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around a threshold. Implies a higher salary helps how you judge your life overall, but won't fix daily dissatisfaction from dull work.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year" (*PNAS*)**
Challenges the plateau finding, showing well-being continues increasing with income for most people. Relevant because it weakens the "money barely matters past comfort" assumption your friends may be leaning on.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" (*PNAS*)**
Reconciles the above: income generally improves well-being, except for an already-unhappy minority. Suggests if the less-interesting job would make you unhappy, more money likely won't compensate.

Bottom line from the literature: interest is important but not *the* sole factor—your desire to lead a team is itself a well-documented source of motivation worth weighing.

rows.answer:
Here are real, relevant papers (the evidence is more "interesting work matters a lot, but so do autonomy and leadership/growth" than "focus on interest almost exclusively"):

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory."**
Introduced the Job Characteristics Model, showing that task variety, autonomy, and experienced meaningfulness strongly drive intrinsic motivation and satisfaction. Notably, autonomy and "growth opportunities"—which leading a team can provide—are core dimensions alongside interest.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis."**
Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only weakly with job satisfaction (~r = .15). This supports your friends' point that "a bit higher salary" should carry little weight in the decision.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being."**
Argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not external rewards—best predict sustained motivation and well-being. This cuts both ways: intrinsic interest matters, but so does the competence/mastery you'd gain from a leadership role you've long wanted.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work."**
People who experience work as a "calling" report higher satisfaction and life meaning, largely independent of income or prestige. Supports prioritizing engaging work, but shows "calling" can also come from a sense of impact/role, not just task content.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins."**
Found that making meaningful progress in one's work is the single strongest driver of positive "inner work life." Relevant because both interest *and* the visible progress of building/leading a team can produce this effect.

One honest caveat beyond the papers: this literature supports that interest beats marginal pay, but it does *not* support ignoring a long-held goal like leading a team—autonomy, mastery, and role meaning are independent satisfaction drivers, so "interest almost exclusively" overstates the evidence.

A quick honest caveat first: the research supports that *interest/meaning is important and often undervalued relative to salary*, but **not** that one should focus on it "almost exclusively." Leading a team can itself satisfy core psychological needs (competence, relatedness, growth). With that framing, here are the relevant works:

**Deci & Ryan (2000) – "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being."** Argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not just interest—drive durable motivation and wellbeing. Note that leading a team strongly serves competence and relatedness, so the "boring" job may score well on these dimensions.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997) – "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work."** Finds that people who view work as a "calling" report higher satisfaction and life meaning than those who see it as a job or career. Supports the idea that subjective engagement matters, but also shows orientation can be cultivated, not just found.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) – "The Progress Principle."** Shows that the single biggest booster of positive "inner work life" is making meaningful progress on work one cares about. Reinforces that intrinsic interest matters—but "progress" can come from new challenges like leadership, not only from inherently fascinating tasks.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976) – "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory."** The Job Characteristics Model identifies five drivers: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. "Interest" maps to only part of this; autonomy and significance (often higher in leadership roles) matter just as much.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010) – "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being."** Money raises life-evaluation but had diminishing emotional returns past a threshold. Relevant because it tempers the value of the salary bump—but see the next paper.

**Killingsworth (2021) – "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year."** Directly challenges the plateau, finding wellbeing continues rising with income. This complicates the "ignore salary, chase interest" claim your friends are making.

**Csikszentmihalyi (1990) – "Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience."** Engagement peaks when challenge matches skill, regardless of the task's surface "interestingness." Suggests a stretch role (managing a team for the first time) could generate flow even if the domain feels less exciting.

Bottom line from the literature: your friends are directionally right that interest is underweighted versus pay, but "almost exclusively" overstates it—autonomy, mastery, growth, and meaning all count, and a leadership stretch may deliver several of those.

Here are real, relevant papers—though I'd flag up front that the actual evidence is more nuanced than "focus almost exclusively on interesting work." Most research supports that intrinsic interest matters *a lot*, but not that other factors (income, autonomy, leadership growth) are negligible.

**Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000). "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation."**
Argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive durable motivation and well-being far more than external rewards. Notably, *competence and autonomy*—which your leadership role might satisfy—are weighted alongside interest, not below it.

**Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999). "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation."**
Found that tangible rewards (like salary bumps) can actually undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. This partially supports your friends, but the effect is about *interesting* work being made boring—not about choosing dull work for money.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz (1997). "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work."**
People who frame work as a "calling" report higher life and job satisfaction regardless of pay or prestige. Crucially, this is partly about *how you construe* the work, not just the work itself.

**Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010). "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being."**
Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaued around \$75k, suggesting more salary buys little extra daily happiness. This is often cited to dismiss money—but see the next paper.

**Killingsworth, M. A. (2021). "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above \$75,000 per Year."**
Directly challenges the plateau: well-being kept rising with income with no ceiling. The salary in your offer may matter more than your friends' "money doesn't count" framing implies.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers (2023). "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved."**
Reconciles the two studies: more money helps most people, but does little for an already-unhappy minority. Translation: if you're content, salary helps modestly; if interesting work is what makes you content, that's the lever.

**Bottom line:** the literature supports interest/intrinsic motivation as a strong predictor of satisfaction, but "almost exclusively" overstates it—autonomy and skill growth (your leadership goal) and income all independently contribute. Your friends are directionally right, not categorically right.

rows.answer:
Here are real, relevant papers—though note up front that the "focus on it almost exclusively" framing overstates what any of this research actually shows.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"** — Foundational self-determination theory work arguing that intrinsic interest drives sustained engagement and well-being. But it stresses three needs together—autonomy, competence, *and* relatedness—not interest alone (leading a team can satisfy the first two).

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the Design of Work"** — The Job Characteristics Model identifies task significance, autonomy, skill variety, and feedback as drivers of satisfaction. "Interestingness" is only one of several ingredients, and the others can be present in a less-interesting-but-more-autonomous leadership role.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings"** — Shows that whether people experience work as a calling depends heavily on the meaning they construct, not just objective job content. Implies your framing of a role matters as much as its inherent "interest."

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"** — Found money raises life satisfaction but emotional well-being plateaus around a threshold. Often cited to downplay salary, but see the correction below.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"** — Reconciles the prior debate, showing well-being generally keeps rising with income for most people. This weakens any strong claim that salary doesn't matter.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Progress Principle"** — Argues that the single biggest day-to-day motivator is making meaningful progress, which interesting work facilitates. But it's about progress and small wins, which a leadership role can also provide.

Bottom line: the research supports intrinsic interest being *important and often underweighted*, but not "almost exclusively"—autonomy, leadership (your stated long-time goal), relatedness, and income all carry real, measured weight.
