rows.answer:
A few genuine, relevant papers—with an honest caveat: I'm not aware of research showing interest should be weighted "almost exclusively," so your friends are overstating a real but partial finding.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS)**
Found that day-to-day emotional well-being stops rising with income above roughly $75k, while life satisfaction keeps climbing. Suggests a raise helps how you judge your life more than how you feel daily.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year" (PNAS)**
Using large real-time data, found well-being keeps increasing with income with no clear plateau. A useful counterweight to over-dismissing the salary bump.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and emotional well-being: A conflict resolved" (PNAS)**
Reconciles the two studies: more money keeps helping most people, but does little for an already-unhappy minority. Implies money's value depends heavily on your baseline and other factors.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
Distinguishes people who see work as a job, career, or calling, with "calling" linked to higher satisfaction. Relevant to whether "interesting" work functions as a calling for you.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory"**
Their Job Characteristics Model identifies skill variety, autonomy, and task significance as drivers of intrinsic motivation. Note that the Google role adds autonomy/leadership, which this model also values—not just "interestingness."

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Intrinsic and Extrinsic Motivations: Classic Definitions and New Directions"**
Self-Determination Theory holds that competence, autonomy, and relatedness sustain motivation. Leading a team can satisfy autonomy and relatedness even if the content is less intrinsically interesting.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Progress Principle"**
Shows that meaningful progress in one's work is the strongest daily booster of motivation and emotion. "Interesting" matters, but so does whether you can make visible progress—possible in both roles.

Bottom line the literature actually supports: interest/intrinsic motivation matters a lot, but so do autonomy, growth (your wish to lead), and income—your friends are right that interest is undervalued, wrong that it should dominate "almost exclusively."

rows.answer:
A few genuinely relevant works—though I'd flag upfront that none support the "almost exclusively" part. The research suggests interest/meaning matters *a lot*, but so do autonomy, growth, and the leadership skills you say you've always wanted.

**Deci & Ryan, "Self-Determination Theory" (2000)**
Argues that intrinsic motivation, fueled by autonomy, competence, and relatedness, predicts persistence and wellbeing better than external rewards. Notably, "competence" and "autonomy" (which a leadership role offers) are weighted alongside interest, not below it.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton, "Crafting a Job" (2001)**
Shows people actively reshape boring roles into meaningful ones through "job crafting," changing tasks and relationships. Implication: a "less interesting" job is partly something you mold, not a fixed property.

**Amabile & Kramer, "The Progress Principle" (2011)**
Finds that the single biggest daily driver of engagement is making progress on meaningful work, not the inherent excitement of the work itself. Leading a team and shipping things can generate exactly this progress signal.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010) and Killingsworth (2021)**
The former found emotional wellbeing plateaus around a moderate income; the latter found it keeps rising with income for most people. Together they suggest a salary bump isn't trivial and shouldn't be dismissed as "just money."

**Gilbert et al., "Affective Forecasting" research (2005)**
Demonstrates people systematically mispredict how happy or unhappy future situations will make them, usually overestimating both. Useful caution: you may be overrating how much the "less interesting" role would actually dull you.

A friendlier read of your friends: interest is underrated *relative to salary alone*. But "lead a small team" is itself a growth and autonomy goal you've stated—so this isn't interesting-vs-boring, it's interesting-vs-(leadership + growth + money). That's a real tradeoff, not a settled one.
